name: Synthetic Response Quality Evaluation Baseline
summary: The staged quality-evaluation baseline finds that synthetic student response quality is evaluated through multiple non-equivalent signals: distributional and embedding realism, downstream training utility, human-response consistency metrics, expert score preservation and realism ratings, profile-to-real-response item alignment, psychometric ability-scale alignment, tutoring-dialogue fidelity metrics, grade-level language and knowledge scope, cognitive authenticity, item-difficulty alignment, and error-pattern plausibility.
main_blockers: The first pass is targeted rather than systematic.

Included evidence spans logical proofs, personalized learning records, essays, multiple-choice item evaluation, NAEP ability simulation, and tutoring dialogue, so one universal quality metric would be misleading.
Several staged sources expose top-line quality methods but need deeper table-level extraction before numeric synthesis.
best_current_signals: Ipci et al. evaluate diversity, realism, embedding/PCA similarity, and downstream grading utility against authentic proof responses.
Agent4Edu evaluates learner-response simulation with consistency and discrepancy metrics against human learner responses.
Wolfe and Barber evaluate simulated essays with expert scores, agreement statistics, distribution comparisons, and realism ratings.
Lu and Wang evaluate generative students through profile alignment, real-response correlation, and overlap in difficult multiple-choice items.
Srivatsa et al. use IRT ability-scale alignment and report that tested LLM proxy students do not reliably reproduce real student ability patterns.
Scarlatos et al. define linguistic, behavioral, and cognitive fidelity metrics for simulated tutoring dialogue students and validate them with human judgments.
Nguyen and Cao evaluate open-ended science idea realism with semantic similarity, grade-level language, knowledge-scope checks, and teacher perceptions across six LLMs.
Cao et al. compare few-shot, fine-tuned, multi-agent, and DPO workflows with researcher-coded cognition and language authenticity for a mathematics simulated learner.
SMART and Wu et al. extend quality evaluation to IRT-aligned item-difficulty prediction and programming-response imperfection/error-pattern realism.
interpretive_note: This baseline maps quality-evaluation families, not a ranking of generation methods. A method can look plausible under one signal, such as believability or distributional similarity, while failing a stricter comparator such as IRT ability alignment or downstream scorer utility.
limitations: The staged source set is a compact baseline-review evidence set, not an exhaustive review.
Quality constructs differ by task: item response, essay quality, dialogue fidelity, profile consistency, and automated-scoring utility are not interchangeable.
Some sources are preprints or rely on abstract-level summary in this staged pass.
This claim does not establish that synthetic responses can replace real or independently human-scored responses for validation.
update_notes: Run a broader database search before treating this as a complete quality-evaluation section.
Extract numeric tables for Lu and Wang, Srivatsa et al., and Scarlatos et al. before any quantitative synthesis.
Separate real-response similarity, human believability, psychometric fidelity, downstream scorer utility, cognitive authenticity, and error-pattern realism in later review bundles.
2026-06-03 claim-hardening update added published validity-condition findings from Nguyen and Cao, Cao et al., SMART, and Wu et al.

supporting_evidence.label: Distributional realism and training utility
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Distributional similarity, embedding-space realism, response diversity, and downstream training utility are one family of quality-evaluation signals.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Ipci et al. evaluate profile-based synthetic responses with diversity, realism, embedding/PCA comparison, and automated-grading training utility.
limitations: This support is bounded to logical-proof response generation.
Downstream training utility is not equivalent to scorer-validation validity.

supporting_evidence.label: Human response consistency and expert scoring
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Generated responses are also evaluated by comparing against human learner responses and expert essay scoring outcomes.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Agent4Edu reports ACC, F1-score, and ROUGE-3 consistency metrics against human learner responses, while Wolfe and Barber use expert scoring, agreement, distribution, and realism checks for simulated essays.
limitations: The Agent4Edu setting is personalized learning simulation.
The Wolfe evidence is essay-specific and preprint-stage.

supporting_evidence.label: Profile alignment and psychometric fidelity
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Profile-item alignment and psychometric ability alignment are different quality constructs and can produce different conclusions about synthetic student fidelity.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Lu and Wang use profile alignment, real-response correlation, and difficult-item overlap for MCQ item evaluation, while Srivatsa et al. use IRT ability-scale alignment and report unreliable LLM proxy-student ability simulation.
limitations: Both sources use item-response settings rather than essays or dialogue.
The staged pass does not extract full numeric tables.

supporting_evidence.label: Dialogue fidelity metrics
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Tutoring dialogue simulation requires multi-dimensional fidelity metrics rather than only item-answer or score-preservation metrics.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Scarlatos et al. define and validate metrics spanning linguistic, behavioral, and cognitive fidelity for simulated students in math tutoring dialogues.
limitations: The staged pass captures the framework and top-line result, not all benchmark values.

supporting_evidence.label: Validity-condition realism metrics
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Recent controlled-comparison studies strengthen the quality-evaluation claim by adding grade-level language, knowledge scope, authenticity coding, IRT difficulty alignment, and error-pattern realism as distinct quality signals.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Nguyen and Cao evaluate science idea realism across six LLMs; Cao et al. evaluate cognition and language authenticity across generation workflows; SMART evaluates IRT-aligned item-difficulty prediction; and Wu et al. evaluate programming-response imperfection and cognitive-level realism.
limitations: The new evidence remains task-bound across science ideas, mathematics simulated learners, item difficulty, and programming responses.
Realism evidence is validity-relevant but still not sufficient for final automated scorer validation.

applies_when: The claim is used to compare quality-evaluation families and their construct boundaries.
does_not_apply_when: One quality signal is treated as sufficient evidence of downstream scorer validity.
unknown_or_unreviewed_contexts: Operational acceptance thresholds for specific assessments.
